data:
Probably from Catania: it was in the Museo dei Benedettini before 1769
                                and the form and content make a Catania provenance most likely.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana
Castello Ursino, Piazza Federico II di Svevia, 95121 Catania (CT)
Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus

G. Kaibel, Inscriptiones Graecae Siciliae et Italiae, additis graecis Galliae Hispaniae, Britanniae, Germaniae inscriptionibus, Inscriptiones Graecae consilio et auctoritate Academiae Litterarum Regiae Borussicae Editae. Volumen XIV., XIV (Berlin: Georgius Reimerus, 1890)
Association pour l’encouragement des études greques, « Bulletin épigraphique », Revue des études grecques, 1888, http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/797735566
Le iscrizioni del Museo civico di Catania : storia delle collezioni, cultura epigrafica, edizione
Kalle Korhonen, Le iscrizioni del Museo civico di Catania : storia delle collezioni, cultura epigrafica, edizione (Helsinki: Societas Scientiarum Fennica, 2004)
Corpus inscriptionum Graecarum IV. Pars XXXIX. Inscriptiones locorum incertorum. Pars XL. Inscriptiones christianae. Indices (1877).

E. Curtius, A. Kirchhoff, and H. Roehl, Corpus inscriptionum Graecarum IV. Pars XXXIX. Inscriptiones locorum incertorum. Pars XL. Inscriptiones christianae. Indices (1877)., vol. 4, 4 vols, Königlich Preussische Akademie der Wissenschaften zu Berlin (Berlin: Ex Officina Academica, 1877)
Silloge di iscrizioni paleocristiane della Sicilia
S.L. Agnello, Silloge di iscrizioni paleocristiane della Sicilia (Rome, 1953)
C. Wessel, Inscriptiones Graecae Christianae Veteres Occidentis, Inscriptiones Christianae Italiae (Bari: Edipuglia, 1989)
Denis Feissel, « Notes d’épigraphie chrétienne (V) », Bulletin de Correspondance Hellénique 105 (1981): 483‑97
Agnello, 1953, Silloge di iscrizioni paleocristiane della Sicilia

Text encoding: Jonathan Prag
1Ἅπασα γε̑α καὶ πλατοὶς ἀὴρ
2γενᾷ σοι θάνατε
3ἐξαπίνης μου τὸ βρέφος ἥρπασε[ς ἦ]
4τίς ἀνάκη εἰ γὰρ ἐγήρα, οὐχεί σον [ἦν]
5((chrismon)) ἐγενήθη ὁ κύρις Ἀγάθων π͡ρ(ὸ) ιε’ ((chrismon))
6καλανδῶν Νοενβρίων ἡμέρᾳ Κρό[νου]
7ἔζη(σεν) μ͡ῆ(νας) ·ι’· ἀπέθανε π͡ρ(ὸ) · ι’·[-?] καλανδῶν Σεπτενβρ[ίων]
8ἡμέρᾳ Ἡλίου κυρία Ἀγάθη εἰρήνην Ἀγά[θωνι]

Text encoding: Jonathan Prag
1ΑΠΑΣΑΓΕ̑ΑΚΑΙΠΛΑΤΟΙΣΑΗΡ
2ΓΕΝΑΣΟΙΘΑΝΑΤΕ
3ΕΞΑΠΙΝΗΣΜΟΥΤΟΒΡΕΦΟΣΗΡΠΑΣΕ[ΣΗ]
4ΤΙΣΑΝΑΚΗΕΙΓΑΡΕΓΗΡΑΟΥΧΕΙΣΟΝ[ΗΝ]
5((CHRISMON))ΕΓΕΝΗΘΗΟΚΥΡΙΣΑΓΑΘΩΝΠ͡Ρ(Ο)ΙΕ((CHRISMON))
6ΚΑΛΑΝΔΩΝΝΟΕΝΒΡΙΩΝΗΜΕΡΑΚΡΟ[ΝΟΥ]
7ΕΖΗ(ΣΕΝ)Μ͡Η(ΝΑΣ)ΙΑΠΕΘΑΝΕΠ͡Ρ(Ο)Ι[-?]ΚΑΛΑΝΔΩΝΣΕΠΤΕΝΒΡ[ΙΩΝ]
8ΗΜΕΡΑΗΛΙΟΥΚΥΡΙΑΑΓΑΘΗΕΙΡΗΝΗΝΑΓΑ[ΘΩΝΙ]

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

English translation

Jonathan Prag

All the earth and the air in its wide expanses begets you, o Death. On a sudden, you snatched away my babe – or was there some necessity, for if he had grown old, would he not still have been yours? Master Agathon was born the 15th day before the Kalends of November (= 18th October), on the day of Kronos; he lived 11 months; he died the tenth day before the Kalends of September (= 23rd August), on the day of the Sun. Lady Agatha, (grant) peace for Agathon!

Italian translation

Serena Agodi

Tutta la terra e il vasto etere generano per te, o Morte. All'improvviso mi hai strappato via il bambino, che necessità c’era? Infatti se fosse invecchiato, non sarebbe stato pur sempre tuo? Il signore Agathon nacque quindici giorni prima delle calende di novembre (= 18 Ottobre) nel giorno di Kronos. Visse undici mesi; morì dieci giorni prima delle calende di settembre (= 23 agosto) nel giorno del Sole. Signora Agatha, (conceda) la pace a Agathon.

commentary

The first four lines are in verse, and express ideas typical in pagan funerary inscriptions; the chi-rho symbols and the latter part of the text show that this is clearly a Christian epitaph. It is rare to mention the date of birth, especially in Greek Christian texts, and most parallels are for infants, as here. The closest parallel is the famous epitaph of Julia Florentina from Catania (now in the Louvre, Paris: Ma2994), an infant who was buried before the shrine of the martyrs, perhaps including St. Agatha. The significance of the final line of the text is debated, but it appears to be a prayer to Saint Agatha, in which case this provides the earliest contemporary evidence for the cult of St. Agatha at Catania, of similar date to that for St. Lucia at Siracusa in the epitaph for Euskia (Museo Archeologico Regionale Paolo Orsi, inv.14600).

  • Agnello 1953 no.106 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10002211
  • Feissel 1981 494 no.xv https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10002010
  • Korhonen 2004 174 https://biblio.inscriptiones.org/epig10000028

Ἅπασα γε̑α καὶ πλατοὶς ἀὴρ γενᾷ σοι θάνατε ἐξαπίνης μου τὸ βρέφος ἥρπασε[ς ἦ] τίς ἀνάκη εἰ γὰρ ἐγήρα, οὐχεί σον [ἦν] ((chrismon)) ἐγενήθη ὁ κύρις Ἀγάθων π͡ρ(ὸ) ιε’ ((chrismon)) καλανδῶν Νοενβρίων ἡμέρᾳ Κρό[νου] ἔζη(σεν) μ͡ῆ(νας) ·ι’· ἀπέθανε π͡ρ(ὸ) · ι’·[-?] καλανδῶν Σεπτενβρ[ίων] ἡμέρᾳ Ἡλίου κυρία Ἀγάθη εἰρήνην Ἀγά[θωνι]

All the earth and the air in its wide expanses begets you, o Death. On a sudden, you snatched away my babe – or was there some necessity, for if he had grown old, would he not still have been yours? Master Agathon was born the 15th day before the Kalends of November (= 18th October), on the day of Kronos; he lived 11 months; he died the tenth day before the Kalends of September (= 23rd August), on the day of the Sun. Lady Agatha, (grant) peace for Agathon!

Tutta la terra e il vasto etere generano per te, o Morte. All'improvviso mi hai strappato via il bambino, che necessità c’era? Infatti se fosse invecchiato, non sarebbe stato pur sempre tuo? Il signore Agathon nacque quindici giorni prima delle calende di novembre (= 18 Ottobre) nel giorno di Kronos. Visse undici mesi; morì dieci giorni prima delle calende di settembre (= 23 agosto) nel giorno del Sole. Signora Agatha, (conceda) la pace a Agathon.

The first four lines are in verse, and express ideas typical in pagan funerary inscriptions; the chi-rho symbols and the latter part of the text show that this is clearly a Christian epitaph. It is rare to mention the date of birth, especially in Greek Christian texts, and most parallels are for infants, as here. The closest parallel is the famous epitaph of Julia Florentina from Catania (now in the Louvre, Paris: Ma2994), an infant who was buried before the shrine of the martyrs, perhaps including St. Agatha. The significance of the final line of the text is debated, but it appears to be a prayer to Saint Agatha, in which case this provides the earliest contemporary evidence for the cult of St. Agatha at Catania, of similar date to that for St. Lucia at Siracusa in the epitaph for Euskia (Museo Archeologico Regionale Paolo Orsi, inv.14600).
